heathlands in the south, where some junipers stand on a low hillock.
name: A Bend in the Trail

desc:
No paths can be found leading past the puddles of muddy water sprinkled among
patches of cotton-grass and hay here. It seems that travellers have avoided this
part of the plains and passed through the heathlands in the north instead. The
ground gets harder in the east, where one can see bushes and taller plants.
Here, only the slender stems ending in a tuft of cotton rise above tussocks of
grass.
name: Wet Plains

desc:
At this part of the plains, the brown and barren land from the west meets with
muddy earth from the north. There are only some wilting plants, while elsewhere
rich ferns, bushes and even a few smaller trees grow. A second look around the
brown plants reveals hard rock below a fine layer of unfertile earth. The water
from rain cannot be stored in that rock, thus making it hard for the vegetation
to conquer all of this land.
name: Between Wet and Dry Plains

desc:
The most remarkable feature of this plain is the brown belt of hard soil which
runs from the north to the south. Dense rocks beneath a thin layer of earth
prevent any larger plants from taking root. Even while some tracks of smaller
animals and an occasional bear can be seen, this plain seems to be desolate.
Small rocks lie scattered in the dust and between clusters of brown grass. To
the east, where the sun rises in the morning, the thin grass gets greener and
greener, until it finally meets the first smaller bushes. From there, it grows
into a high meadow.
name: Thin Grass

desc:
This hard soil has seen a big fire not long ago. The formerly dry grass has
burned down as well as the few bushes from which only a few blackened and
crumbled remains stick out of the soil. To the east, the plain becomes greener
and moist, so the fire was forced to stop there. The remains of an old campfire
lie in the middle of the ash-covered plain. At the borders of the great burning,
nature is just starting to reclaim what was lost, but to the north, the south
and the west, the ground does not seem to be fertile enough for fast
regeneration.
name: A Scorched Plain

desc:
In the northern part, the plain is scorched and black, and only some grass has
just started to grow again. Obviously, the burning stopped when it reached a
broad girdle of stone and rock where only a few thistles could take root - not
enough for a fire to spread further. Behind that girdle to the south, the plain
becomes covered with medium high, yellow and green grass which hides many little
holes dug by gophers, rabbits or similar creatures. Little piles of loose rocks,
maybe of the size of a halfling's fist, are scattered over on this plain. With
all these rocks and holes around, any rider is forced to dismount.
name: A Girdle of Stone

desc:
Grass and a few low bushes cover the northern plain while, the vegetation to the
east and the south gets much denser. Travelling here is not hindered, but it
does not seem to be possible to pass through the high row of bushes which
stretches from the northeast to the southwest. All around there, are signs of
animal life to be recognised, as nests and droppings of birds or the rustling of
rabbits and other beasts, too small to be spotted among the high and bent grass.
name: An Overgrown Plain

desc:
Groups of large bushes, high grass and the occasional cluster of ferns cover
this plain. There are a lot of rocks and stones here hidden by the leaves and
the grass coloured in all of the many shades of green and yellow. It is
impossible to cross this place while riding, as any mount could break a leg here
among the rocks too easily. To the south, a white pile of rocks rises from the
plain, while the ground has less vegetation. The soil there seems to be drier
than in the surrounding areas. Many small cracks run through it and vanish down
into the soil.
name: Dense Bushes

desc:
Many trails have been trampled into the rich ferns and the high grass, leading
through and around bushes to a small, nearly completely overgrown pond. In the
soft mud, many imprints of various animals can be seen. Next to the pond, there
lie three big rocks, all half sunk into the soil, as if some giant had placed
them here for the weary traveller to rest on.
name: A Pond in the Overgrown Plain

desc:
Dense ferns cover the plains, hiding every part of the soft soil. The light
green of the fern is only broken by the round stems of trees, which stretch
their branches six to twenty feet above the ground. A few paths created by all
kinds of animals appear here and there in the thick green, meander through the
underbrush before disappearing out of sight.
name: Fern Covered Plains

desc:
Grassy fields and thick ferns cover this end of the plains. In the east, tall
trees rise above a tangled shrubbery, marking the outskirts of a forest. The
ground slopes gently towards a heath in the north. Some dry stalks of tall
plants stand in the grass, spreading their seeds to the wind.
name: Plains Bordering the Forest

desc:
Several juniper trees cover a low hillock in the heath, adding greenery to the
otherwise barren landscape. Some juniper branches have been cut off with a sharp
object; someone passed through here, but the markings are more than a few days
old. In the east, the heath turns into a field of weeds, and in the north, a
trail is winding through a group of tall trees.
name: A Small Stand of Junipers

desc:
Clusters of smaller trees stand scattered to the north, while to the south, a
dense row of bushes grows. Between these, there is not much diversion for the
eye, since only high grass grows there. The trees themselves are young oaks with
dark brown trunks, while the bushes are partly thorny but mostly thick and old
so that they even block the path to the south.
name: At a Row of Bushes

desc:
Layers of dried leaves cover the floor of this oak forest with a swathe of
dulled red and greyish orange. The trees here have seen numerous autumns and
springs, but now their time seems to have come to an end. The trees stand still,
tall and unbowed. Yet, their successors can already be seen: Young birches grow
scattered all over this part of the forest and wait for their time to come. One
day, a forest of birches is likely to grow in this place.
name: A Withering Oak Forest

desc:
Wild apple trees grow here. Each of them has its own area of influence where its
wide branches throw their shadows. The trees spread as far as the eye can see on
this plain. A huge row of dense bushes grows from the north to the west, and
probably this row has caused just the right circumstances for this wild orchard
to prosper. Low, yet very thick grass covers the soil and forms a soft carpet.
name: Wild Apple Trees

desc:
A deep chasm cleaves this plain in two to the east. Like a deep wound, it
extends from the far north to the south. The plain grass covered with thin grass
abruptly ends and vanishes into the clammy and cold depth. It seems possible but
dangerous to climb down with the help of some protrusions, cracks and some thin
plants, which have been able to find some firm spots for their roots in the
steep and moist walls. The far side of this chasm seems to offer some more
foothold for growing things, since it is covered with loose tendrils and maybe
even the occasional wild cherry tree or young birch.
name: Before a Deep Chasm

desc:
The craggy ground, uneven and covered with rocks of all shapes and some twisted
bushes, makes this plain impossible to ride on. Even some cracks run through
this plain, and many of them are only partly filled with earth. Grass and some
trees grow out of these cracks, and some dirty puddles of mud have formed all
around. Whether this ragged area has something to do with the deep chasm to the
east or not can not be said for sure, yet it is quite likely. It seems as if
many decades or even centuries ago a strong earthquake had ripped this plain in
two. In the northern parts, more and more trees take root in the soil and grow
high above the grassland.
name: Craggy Ground in the Plains

desc:
Many old birches grown high in the sky, their leaves and branches filter the
light of sun, moon and stars. All around, there can be seen the white and thick
stems like thousands of vertical lines. The floor is covered with soft grass and
thick moss. The lack of noise makes this a very quiet part of the forest.
name: A Forest of Old Birches

desc:
While only sparse trees cover this plain, many bushes of all kinds seem to
prosper all around. They are not dense enough to hinder any movement or sight
and grow here in a wide variety. Some have long and thin leaves, while others
seem quite broad and their leaves are from a more blue than yellow shade of
green, or their stems seem to be as thick as smaller trees. In between these
bushes, low grass covers the ground in which animals have left many paths,
leading from or towards the forest to the west and the north.
name: Sparse Trees and Bushes

desc:
An ancient oak stands here all alone near a deep chasm. Only soft grass covers
the plain, and the next bush or tree can only be seen in quite a distance from
the enormous tree in the centre. Its old, broad branches cast a huge shadow and
loom over the abyss to the west. Under it, many mounds dug by moles can be seen.
Its crown looks as if only the strongest of storms were able to move it and to
force it to move out of the position it is used to.
name: An Ancient Oak

desc:
Half a dozen poles have been set firmly into the earth. They are all covered
with old and dried blood. The poles now have a brownish, encrusted surface. The
grass around this spot is dark and blackened in a wide radius. Not much damage
has been caused to the formerly thick grass and the bushes around, it may have
been a bit trampled, but not enough to explain this dying area. To the west, a
deep chasm blocks the way in this direction. Many decades ago, a very heavy
earthquake seems to have torn this plain literally asunder.
name: At Some Blood-encrusted Poles

desc:
High, tree-like bushes and a few isolated trees dominate the vegetation, and
hawthorn stands out against a green background in some places. Forming a dense
undergrowth interlocked in the bushes, ling patches cover the brown soil rich in
humus, shielding it against erosion from rainfall and wind which both seem to be
common here.
name: Bushy Plains

desc:
Nothing but thick grass covers the windy landscape. The grass is not very high,
making hiding rather hard, and since it is sharp, it also makes travelling
unpleasant in this area. It looks as if wildlife mostly consisted of birds and
rodents, judging from the sounds that can be heard occasionally. Here and there,
smooth dunes rise from the ground.
name: Lush Grass

desc:
The high grass here is trampled down by many passing animals, perhaps seeking
food and water, forming a small, vaguely recognisable path. The common presence
of small animals has left its marks, as many remains can be discerned. A tiny
bit of fur or a strand of hair hanging on a thorny bush, or piles of dried
excrements near the path can be noticed on a second glance.
name: Animal Path

desc:
The cave is large, yet not very high. The floor is covered with dry soil and
near the rear end, it is padded with straw. A sharp smell hangs in the air.
Trampled grass still grows near its entrance, and bones of animals lie here and
there on the ground. Some of them are picked dry, others still have remains of
rotting meat on them.
name: A Wolf Den

desc:
It looks as if lots of animals passed here on usual basis, travelling from the
north-east to the south-west, and vice versa. The path is partly hidden among
the high grass surrounding it, but once found, its course is clearly visible.
Along it, the grass is trampled down, the earth is partially bare where too many
animals have wandered, and several little bushes are broken. Many animal tracks
can clearly be seen on the ground even by the most unskilled.
name: Animal Track

desc:
An intangible sense of rot and decay hangs here, making this barren land similar
to a carrion feeding ground. Even though this place is located near a cliff
visible to the south-east, the air still feels stale, and only the sound of a
few insects gives the impression as if it were filled with anger. The vegetation
of the surrounding land very abruptly ends. Close to the middle of this ground,
a few tiny rocky mounds arise.
name: Cursed Ground

desc:
Due to a caprice of nature, a quartz deposit which normally lies deep under the
rich and dark earth has come to the surface, mixing with it and being home to a
few plants only. Short and thin grass and some erica patches grow here and
there, and in a few places where the sand seems to have mixes less with soil,
even a few squat bushes manage to grow, while the majority of the ground is
lying idle, although there seems to be sufficient moisture.
name: Sandy Prairie

desc:
Protected by a small circle of stones in the surrounding ground, a marshy dip
filled with reeds, flowers and fungi can be noticed. Around the dip, the soil
seems to be very fertile for a while, as it is covered with long, golden grass
and a few, solitary trees.
name: Sheltered Dip in the Plains

desc:
Some time in the past, a fierce brushfire must have raged here, burning to ash
most of the vegetation. Small and coaled trunks, partly uproot, are sad remains
of what once were trees. Only a thin layer of moss and grass covers the ground,
rejuvenating the partly cracked, partly grey ground, loosening the soil and
preparing the rebirth of the flora.
name: Burnt Prairie

desc:
The land is covered with woody, low grass and a few tree-like bushes. A few
patches of ling grow here and there, and a strong smell, coming from large
clusters of oregano, fills the air. The soil is brown and crumbly, and traces of
white sand can be noticed here and there among it.
name: Grassy Prairie

desc:
Made uneven over years of exposure to the power of constant winds and rain, the
ground here is uneven. Long, grass-covered ridges and small water-filled gullies
form the landscape, and heather and sturdy bushes are the dominating plants.
Large boulders brought over time from rivers stand like attached sentinels,
overlooking the plains.
name: Cracked Ground

desc:
Fresh grass covers the soil here like a bright, green and brown blanket. The
soil is coarse, sticking together as if bound by an unseen glue, making it form
large chunks at many places. The peaks of a few large boulders and rocks,
remains from ancient times, protrude through it.
name: Depressed Land

desc:
On the grassy, flat area, a huge, several hundred years old oak with a wide
crown is towering over the plains below. Obviously, in the past, other trees
were growing here as well, but now nothing except for a few rotting stumps has
remained of them. Wild rose bushes grow in the proximity of the tree, and many
flowers bloom on the ground.
name: Bottom of an Ancient Oak

desc:
A blood-red streak of clay, nearly a hundred yards long, cuts through this land
like a wound on Arda herself. Sun and wind have dried out parts of it, making
these spots hard and cracked. Spindly grey-green plants writhe in the wind
around the clay gash, while moss partially grows on the clay vein. The land is
flat and uniform in all directions, but it seems as if in some distance, the
earth became less barren.
name: A Clay-veined Wasteland

desc:
The ground is soft and damp here, covered with a rainbow of various fungi and
some shallow grass. In the middle of this area in a shallow dip, a muddy pond
has developed from some sort of underground source of water, flowing only a
small distance to the southwest where it disappears into the ground. The earth
near the basin is marshy, showing obvious tracks of various animals.
name: Watering Hole

desc:
Almost resembling a well-disciplined army, squat, widely cantilevered and woody
hawthorn bushes grow in well-ordered rows. The earth seems to be a thick layer
over a more rocky, solid base, and the tinge of rich soil in the air and the
number of shrubs growing suggests that these plains are more than suitable for
plant life. The odour of fresh water adds the impression that moisture is in
abundance in the area.
name: Field of Hawthorns

desc:
Tall grass and young saplings grow indomitably here, giving the area the
imression of the wilderness that it is, interrupted by large patches of heather.
A few trees grow scattered in the grassland, with a tendency of becoming
stronger in numbers and in size, but also changing in kind in the western parts
of the plains.
name: Wooded Plains

desc:
A dense forest of conifers, like firs or pines, gives shelter from wind and
light rain. The grass grows high and mixes with the lower branches. Only the
closest or loudest noises can be heard, everything else gets somewhat damped.
Low tunnels have been formed in the grass by smaller animals like rabbits, foxes
or the thistly hedgehog.
name: A Forest of Conifers

desc:
The top of the high oak provides a natural vantage point for observing the
surrounding land. The branches of the ancient tree are sturdy enough to support
several men at once, and since they diverge in all directions, they form a
natural platform. During the seasons from spring to fall, lush leafage hangs
from them, providing shadow and an excellent place to hide.
name: Crown of an Ancient Oak

desc:
Thick, round bushes cover the fields of hay at the border of a forest. Young
trees rise above the bushes here and there, their chewed bark and branches
hinting at an abundance of local game. Several animal paths run amidst the
bushes, showing an occasional track or a dropping on the hard-packed soil. The
twists and bends in the paths are well concealed by the growth, but most of them
seem to be leading from the open plains in the east towards the forest in the
west.
name: Animal Paths in the Bushes

desc:
This deep pit has been dug straight into the ground, and is surrounded from
above by some bushes. The walls of the pit are almost vertical and offer no good
handholds, making climbing out of the pit quite hard. Sharpened, sturdy sticks
have been placed at the bottom of the pit, where they point upwards waiting for
some hapless victim to fall on them. The trap is crude, but its efficiency is
without question.
name: A Spiked Pit

desc:
Ferns and mosses of all kinds grow in the moist soil. The ground in the
depression is not as hard as on the fields surrounding it, hinting at the
presence of water. The undergrowth is very dense, proving the earth to be very
nutritious in addition to being rather moist. The few rocks here are covered in
lichen of sickly green colour.
name: Soggy Depression

desc:
Some clear water wells up from a soft, dark mound on the ground, announcing the
presence of a spring. It forms a small pool before flowing to the west and being
soaked into the sand. Stones in different sizes cover the ground, making it
seemingly difficult to tread on, but no other features to hinder travel can be
seen in the open landscape. A rise in the plains towards the east is noticeable,
as well as soggy plains to the west and the dry field to the north.
name: A Spring in the Stony Plains

desc:
Surrounded by similarly open plains, this field of weeds is nothing remarkable.
Some small stones lie on the bottom of the occasional depression here, as if
they had rolled in from somewhere. The landscape turns slowly barren towards the
south, where the hue of the weeds becomes completely brown. The fields in the
north seem more fertile.
name: Undulating Fields of Yellowed Weeds

desc:
Light patches of moisture can be seen on the ground at all times, arising from
the spongy soil that forms the surface below. The ground is covered with weeds
growing completely wild, yet there are some spots where vegetation has not
settled in permanently. There, fungus-infested branches and rotting leaves can
be seen on the ground, showing how the old makes way for the new in the eternal
cycle of seasons.
name: Thriving Weeds

desc:
For no obvious reasons the ground here is dead. As if it had not rained for
months, grass looks burnt and worn out. Fine grey dust rises from the ground,
covering the leaves of what little vegetation is left around here. The lands
surrounding these fields seem to have had more rainfall recently, since lush
grasses dominate the landscape farther away.
name: Dusty Plains

desc:
This area may have been a farmland many years ago, but it seems more likely that
some grains of wheat ended up here by chance and grew into a small field. The
wheat has grown wild among the local weeds and could not be harvested easily.
Still, it supplies the occasional grazing animal with food that will last long
into the winter, even under the snow. The fields extend featurelessly away from
this patch.
name: Wild Field of Wheat

desc:
A great brushfire seems to have raged in the western part of this forest some
time ago. Still, the old trees here have remained unharmed by the scorching
flames, even if some of their trunks are slightly soot-covered. The ground is
covered with dense vegetation and clumped roots extending from the trees,
hinting at the fertility of the soil and making the terrain difficult to travel.
Twilight enshrouds the thick foliage and a hollow sigh echoes through the dark
trees whenever the wind blows.
name: A Gloomy Corner of the Forest

desc:
Lying just north of a forest, this huge field still shows no sign of the trees
or heavy undergrowth that can be found there. Beside a few bushes, all visible
vegetation is grass, which covers the rocky ground with a smooth yellowish green
layer. Odd shapes, like rusty shards of metal, stick out of the ground here and
there hinting at some battle fought here a long time ago.
name: A Grassy Field

desc:
A lonely grave mound, located among some dry bushes, catches the eye in this
grassy field. The mound is large enough to contain a few man-sized bodies, or
perhaps that of one large beast. No signs of habitation in these lands are
visible, only faint depressions in the grass hint at something passing through
here often. To the north, the grassy field turns into rolling plains.
name: A Lonely Mound in the Grassy Fields

desc:
The smooth carpet of grass is broken by a wide, sandy pit. A narrow dirt path
leads from the north to the edge of the pit, where someone has built simple
wooden stairs to make climbing up and down the sand-covered slope easier. At the
bottom of the pit, a blackened skewer hangs above a bed of burnt logs and
stumps. The old, burned down fire is surrounded by shreds of charred skin and
gnawed bones, giving some hints on what the hunters' latest catch might have
been. Judging from the amount of leftovers on the ground, it is no small group
that enjoys their meals here.
name: At a Roasting Pit

desc:
Far away from all inhabited lands, the weeds on these fields have been growing
without any limitations. Grass of medium height covers the ground in all
directions and grows thicker to the north. Small paths lead through the grass,
making movement easier. Neither grass nor rocks can be seen to add colour into
the constant brown of their worn surface of dirt.
name: Paths in the Grass

desc:
A variety of grasses have grown tall on this field, making movement on anything
but the narrow animal paths quite hard. At the sides of the paths, hummocks of
tough weeds rise from the ground, and one can spot several places where animals
have been digging for roots to eat. One path going west seems wider than the
others, suggesting it is used by wandering herds or perhaps even hunters or
travellers.
name: Fields of Tall Grasses

desc:
The ground here is notably lower than in the surrounding meadows. For anyone
standing on the bottom of the hollow, there is no way of seeing if someone is
approaching. Being protected from the wind however, the hollow offers a
sheltered place for listening the sounds of the plains. Some dropping-covered
turf at the inner side of the hollow suggests this is also the home of some
large animal.
name: A Dip in the Meadows

desc:
Tall bushes rise high above the ground, standing in winding rows and forming
twisting corridors where one can pass between them. The lower branches of the
bushes are leafless, while those near their tops spread out and create a green
ceiling. The ground is bare and soft, even slightly muddy. Many kinds of vines
seem to thrive here, as they can be often seen creeping up along the shrubs.
name: Bushes Bordering the Forest

desc:
This slight depression in the middle of a field of sand seems to be a frequently
used place for camping. A lonely tree on one side of it offers some protection
from the elements under its branches, where someone has built a small shelter.
The crude structure is made of pruned young trees bound together tightly with
some lengths of rope, and seems adequate for spending a night or two. A sooty
pit near the shelter, obviously the spot for a campfire, is surrounded by large
stones to keep some of the heat.
name: Sand-covered Campsite

desc:
As if planted by hand to show the beauty of Arda, this meadow contains a variety
of flowers. Bushes of eglantine are spreading their lush branches at some spots,
while on the ground one can see anemones and asphodels growing in the shade.
Some irises grow in the soft mud along a few ditches, where also the footprints
of different animals coming here show easily. The ground near the clusters of
flowers is rather flat but begins to slope farther away from this field. In the
north, the landscape seems much more barren.
name: A Meadow of Flowers

desc:
This crude outlook built of logs and long planks can be seen from far away on
these open fields. It is only about a dozen feet high, but standing on the
seemingly highest hillock anywhere within several miles, it could grant an
impressive view over the surrounding grasslands. The bushes growing below the
wooden stand fail to hide the ladder leading up from view; it seems whoever
built the structure had little concern for secrecy here.
name: Outlook in the Plains

desc:
Here and there, green tufts of grass peek out of the sand covering these wide
fields. The landscape is open with hardly any trees, allowing one to see far in
every direction. The season's weather has left its mark on the smooth ground,
where wavy patterns decorate the surface. Among them, an occasional animal
track, even that of a snake, is visible.
name: Fields of Sand

desc:
A wide mound, several feet high, rises above the fields of sand here. The top of
the mound is covered by a carpet of tangled, dry grass, while on its sides
nothing seems to hold the dirt in place. Some old stumps stick out of the
northern side of the mound; perhaps this area was once part of a great forest.
The open field extends to the south, where no signs of vegetation can be seen.
name: A Sandy Mound

desc:
This outlook post has been constructed of old timbers which seem sturdy enough
to endure the years of standing on this open hilltop. Wide planks make up the
floor of the platform, where some gaps and holes offer a glimpse of the bushy
ground below. There is no ceiling, so this place was most likely not meant to
work as a shelter. However, it seems to be well suited for serving as a
watchtower: the view over the surrounding landscape is remarkable.
name: Up in the Outlook Post

desc:
The ground slopes up and down, but is still plain enough not to hinder any
movement. Anyone wanting to travel fast between the vales of Anduin and the
southeastern expanse, could take a quick ride over these plains. Other than a
distant tree standing in the west, and what seems to be an outlook tower far in
the east, no remarkable features can be seen in the landscape.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
The ground slopes slightly to the west, down towards the Anduin River, giving a
good view of the wide fields spreading out there. The ground is covered with
grass and clover, which has grown unusually tall. Some round bumps on the
slopes, crowned by this vegetation, make the fields appear sheltered despite the
lack of trees and bushes. Here and there, small rabbit holes can be found.
name: Sloping Meadows

desc:
Thorny thickets cover the ground, but the vegetation has not grown high enough
to hinder any movement. The shrubs stand almost motionless in this open
landscape, since their tough branches could only be swayed by the strongest of
winds. The hay growing here is light, and passing through the fields is easy
along the numerous animal paths leading to the west. A slope to the east leads
up to a meadow and a great oak can be seen to the south.
name: Sparse Thickets in the Hay

desc:
Rising well above thirty feet and being about twice as broad from branch to
branch, a great oak dominates the field. Its thick branches are covered with
huge leaves, offering a shelter against most kinds of weather, while the nooks
and crannies among its roots and on its burly trunk give the same for small
animals. The grassy plains around the oak are flat and to the east they extend
towards the distant Mirkwood forest.
name: Grassy Plains by a Great Oak

desc:
A field of fine sand spreads out over the open landscape. Occasional stunted
trees seem to be the most visible landmarks around here, while hummocks of weeds
with long leaves make up most of the vegetation on the ground. No signs of water
are visible in the area, yet a number of holes and burrows in the sand hint at
an abundance of animal life.
name: Weeds in the Sand

desc:
The dry soil here seems to offer little nourishment for the local plantlife, yet
some thin bushes with round, leathery leaves manage to thrive on these otherwise
open plains. Their branches are quite crooked and their bark grey, making it
hard to guess their age, while the rich green hue of their leaves suggests they
could survive for many years to come. The bushes mark a noticeable transition
between the verdant grasslands to the west and a wide, sandy area to the east.
name: Thin Bushes

desc:
Most of the fields here are covered with low but thick green grass. Only a small
thicket of globular bushes with tiny sharp leaves breaks the monotony of these
fields overlooking an ancient trail in the north. These plains are often
inhabited by many forms of wildlife, as one can tell from the chewed twigs and
blades of grass.
name: Grasslands

desc:
Following the course of Anduin as it turns westwards, the worn trail continues
to pass through plains and grassland. Only a lonely brushwood growing on the
eastern side of the trail differs from the surrounding landscape. White, small
pebbles lie on and around the trail's path.
name: Worn Pebble Trail

desc:
Thick bushes with sharp thorns grow wild in this small valley. The layer of fine
sand covering the valley floor seems to offer little nourishment for other
vegetation, allowing one to move rather freely in places where the bushes have
not yet spread into. Some animal paths lead up and down the surrounding slopes,
showing where travelling through is easiest. A low hollow lies in the middle of
the valley, perhaps marking the spot of a now dry pond.
name: Amidst the Thorny Shrubs

desc:
The landscape descends steeply towards the Great River in the north. In some
places, where the weather has worn out the carpet of grass, loose soil and
pebbles are slowly spreading over the slope. Some blackberry bushes grow at
spots where the soil is still rich, sinking their roots into the layer of gravel
and holding it in place. Tiny trickles of water run down the bedrock in the few
places where it is visible, indicating that some groundwater might break through
the surface here.
name: Sloping Fields of Blackberry Bushes

desc:
Sharply the worn trail turns west, passing an ancient milestone with faint
markings on its rough surface. Once, a long time ago, this place used to be a
crossroad for two trails. Unused in the recent years, the east going trail
quickly disappears under the soft mat of grass. The main trail comes from the
north and continues westward, while east and south from this point lie plains of
green grass and occasional bushes.
name: A Bend in the Worn Trail

desc:
Moving closer to the river the trail reaches a place where many animals, small
and big, come to refresh themselves with the flowing water. Because of a thick
layer of limestone in the ground, the soil is as hard as rock. It is likely this
is why there are almost no plants growing around here.
name: Worn Trail by the Anduin

desc:
Being considerably lower than the surrounding fields, meadows and plains, this
valley is only slightly above the water level of the Anduin. The riverside edge
of the valley is covered with dark yellow reeds and the ground gets rather wet
and swampy. It seems this valley is usually flooded at the high-water periods in
the springtime.
name: Sloping Valley

desc:
A low hillock, shaped like a bowl turned upside down, rises above the fields. A
small wood of bushy pines stands on top of the slope, hiding the lands to the
east behind them. An ancient trail travels through the terrain along the river
Anduin, bending slightly on its course where it meets the pine-covered hillock.
The surface of the trail consists of hard-packed dirt with some loose pebbles,
making it seem like an easy route to travel to the north and west.
name: A Bend in the Trail along the Anduin

desc:
Here, the ancient trail following the Anduin narrows considerably as it is
engulfed in a shrubbery of willows and other bushes with slender branches. The
ground is furrowed amidst the bushes, showing signs of water having formed ponds
and ditches or flown towards the river. The trail winds past dense shrubs and
deep hollows as it continues out of the shrubbery and towards more open land.
name: Winding Trail amidst a Shrubbery

desc:
Passing through fertile grasslands and meadows, the trail reaches an area of
less greenery. While some grass is still visible, much of the ground is covered
with stones. A thick green mat of moss grows all around, even on the largest
rocks. Right next to the trail, some rocks have tangles of vines with small
leaves growing on them.
name: Wide Trail through the Meadows

desc:
This is a perfect place to get a good sight over the Anduin river flowing by. An
about two feet high bank, falling straight down into the river, is covered with
smaller plants only, so the view of the Anduin is remarkable. Farther away from
the river the grass is higher and even a few bushes appear. Signs of small
animals living around here are visible in the undergrowth.
name: Soft Riverbank

desc:
Rocks of various sizes and shapes dot the smooth grass at this southern bank of
the river Anduin. A chain of knolls runs along the river, offering a few good
places to see the swirling waters below, while level grassland continues away
from the river. The vegetation is sparse here, but some bushes begin to show far
in the south. A pyramidal pile of rocks sits on the top of the highest knoll
around.
name: At the Riverside

desc:
A swift stream of cold, clear water babbles cheerfully as it breaks through the
layer of rolling pebbles covering the ground, creating a shallow yet noticeable
obstacle. The rivulet seems to enter this low point in the landscape from the
east, following the trail for a while, then making a swift turn across it and
towards the river Anduin. Slender saplings grow here and there along the stream,
which seems to carry along occasional twigs, leaves and seeds, helping the local
vegetation to spread.
name: A Rivulet across the Trail

desc:
An ancient trail passes by a bend in the Anduin, offering a view of the banks on
this side of the river. Steep, grassy slopes descend towards the water, which
seems quite calm below this point, while surges and whirlpools appear farther
away where some rocks stand out. The trail continues to follow the river to the
west and to the north, while from the east, a narrow rivulet slithers down the
slopes to meet it.
name: Riverbend Trail

desc:
This edge of the forest is quite open since the trees stand far apart and their
thin branches offer little shade. Clusters of colourful flowers and clumps of
wriggling roots enliven the ground under the tall birches, whose white trunks
reach for the skies like pillars of finest marble. Moss-covered cliffs rise
among the trees and thickening vegetation in the east, where the forest seems to
get much darker.
name: In the Sparse Forest

desc:
A clear stream flows gently through the mixed forest, carrying fallen leaves and
broken stems among the quietly swirling waters. Bushes with hanging branches
outline its sides; here and there a fallen trunk has pushed them aside, forming
a natural bridge over the stream. The looming shapes of various tall trees are
reflected from the almost still surface of the water, until it begins to ripple
considerably towards the west.
name: Forest Stream

desc:
A steep face of rock, about six feet high, separates these sloping fields from
the forested area to the east. A gap in the small wood standing above the ridge
reveals a clear rivulet, which gains speed as it meets the sudden fall. The
sounds of water breaking against stone at the bottom of the waterfall can be
heard even from a distance, and small droplets hang on the leaves of the
surrounding vegetation. The stream continues down to the northwest where the
river Anduin lies in the distance.
name: At a Small Waterfall

desc:
The dry, waist-high hay in these fields hides every rock and stump from view.
Amidst the hay some old tangled nests of small birds can be noticed. In some
places, the hay has been flattened either by rain or by large animals following
their paths through the plains. From here, wide grasslands spread out to the
east.
name: Dry Plains of Hay

desc:
A carpet of grass covers the gentle slope leading up from a wide hollow in the
west. Bushes are scarce here and the only place for animals to hide in seems to
be the high hay in the north. The plains continue as far as the eye can see to
the southeast.
name: On a Gentle, Grassy Slope

desc:
Rocks about the size of a man's head, covered in black and green lichen, lie
about everywhere on this field. The ground must be quite dry since only tiny
flowers, sprigs and weeds grow in the otherwise smooth fields of yellowed grass.
The hay gets higher in the east, but even there the only source of water seems
to be rain. Some tall rowans stand in the middle of the fields, providing
berries for local birds even in the winter.
name: In a Rocky Field

desc:
A wide hollow in the plains separates a rocky area in the north from grasslands
extending to the other directions. Water is seeping from an embankment, forming
a puddle at the bottom of the hollow. From there the water flows westwards,
turning from a narrow stream into a rivulet near a pair of boulders. The rivulet
soon disappears from sight as it runs into some dense vegetation.
name: A Narrow Rivulet through the Plains

desc:
Thick bushes grow here and there in a field of weeds, flanking the furrow of a
rivulet running towards the river. The ground seems somewhat muddy, but not too
soft to cross. Some young trees stand among mossy boulders in the north where
the ground slopes upwards to meet a low hillock of bare rock. Thick shrubbery
continues in the east, but farther south the bushy area seems to turn into an
open meadow.
name: Sparse Bushes near the River

desc:
Water flows in trickles through the sand where a rivulet joins the great river
Anduin. A narrow trail following the river leads over the beach, where crossing
the small stream seems easier, then disappears back into the surrounding
brushwood. It seems possible to follow the rivulet eastwards; the vegetation
gets less dense on higher grounds. Some droppings can be noticed near the
stream, suggesting that wild animals come here for water.
name: The Trail Passing a Sandy Beach

desc:
Patches of moss covering the ground leave the rock bare only where the trail
climbs over a low cliff. Young birches growing on all sides of this rocky
outcrop make room for the trail as it goes up towards higher cliffs in the west
and downhill in the north. Grassy plains extend eastwards, and the ground slopes
steeply down to moist fields in the south.
name: On the Trail over a Small Cliff

desc:
Tussocks of weeds and grass make the ground seem soft to tread on. Some larger
rocks lie hidden under flattened hay, but avoiding such obstacles would be easy
on these open fields. The ground slopes steeply to the east where one might get
a better look over the plains. Small bushes grow here and there, gathering into
a hedgerow far in the north.
name: A Lush Meadow of Weeds

desc:
A single boulder stands on top of a grassy incline, marking the highest point in
the plains. If this was anywhere near a populated area, there would probably be
a tale about how the boulder got there. But here, it is surrounded only by
desolate grasslands stretching as far as the eye can see.
name: High Point in the Plains

desc:
The blackened trunk of a long-dead tree stands tall in the middle of a field of
hay. The wood is splintered and hard as rock near the ground; perhaps a
lightning struck here a long time ago, setting fire to the tree and burning away
the rest of the forest. There seems to be a hole under the roots of the tree,
but it is covered by a tangle of dead branches. In the south, a stand of shorter
but living trees forms the outskirts of a forest.
name: A Lonely Dead Tree

desc:
The young birches growing in this part of the forest cannot be older than a
decade, but many charred stumps of once mighty trees lie on the ground among
rocks and weeds. In the south, the rocky furrow of a dried-up stream separates
this part of the forest from dense brushwood. All around, the tiny holes of some
rodents can be seen in the nooks under the stumps. The growth of birches becomes
much more dense towards the east and south, where the wall of branches hinders
visibility greatly.
name: In a Thicket of Birches

desc:
Short grass, wild strawberries, and small plants with large leaves cover this
small field. Three narrow dirt paths meet in the middle of the field and
continue to the door of a cabin standing near the outskirts of a forest. In the
south, a thick shrubbery engulfs the grass, and also some taller trees begin to
show in that direction.
name: Field in Front of a Small Cabin

desc:
There is enough room inside this lodge for several travellers to sleep on the
dirt floor. A niche in the middle of the cabin seems to have held campfires in
the past, and there is a slit in the roof made of sticks to lead any smoke out.
Walls build of thick logs and sealed with moss keep the wind out, leaving a
musty smell to the air inside. Two broken shelves hanging from the back wall are
the only pieces of furniture in the lodge.
name: Inside the Musty Lodge

desc:
Thick shrubs under the trees make travelling through this end of the forest
rather hard. Stinging nettles among the undergrowth make the woods seem
unfriendly and seldom visited, yet a dirt path leads north towards what seems to
be a small cabin near the edge of the forest. In the south, the path quickly
disappears under dense shrubs and tall, looming oaks. Deeper in the forest, the
huge trees seem to keep everything under a constant shadow.
name: Dense Vegetation among the Trees

desc:
Short stalks of grass intermingle with gnarled walnut trees and hoary ashes in
this small forest. Although bursting with life, the forest is mostly silent. The
grassy ground underfoot is damp and gives forth a fresh vigour. All over this
forest, small patches of light blue flowers can be seen.
name: A Quiet Forest

desc:
Low grass suddenly turns into a field of waist-high goldenrods, which make a
whooshing noise whenever a gust of wind ploughs its way through them. In the
west, a small cabin can be seen near the edge of the forest that spreads around
the field. The woods in the east seem less dense, while in the north open
grassland stretches over a steep slope.
name: Field of Goldenrods

desc:
Through the clean water the bottom of the riverbed is visible. It is covered
with sharp-edged stones and some higher formations of rock. Some of that rock
even reaches the surface, making this part of the river rather risky to pass for
an unwary traveller. With some skill and care however, it seems possible to take
even a large boat through here.
name: The Anduin River

desc:
A steep face of rock rises in the south, causing a slight change of course to
the Great River. The flow becomes rapid as the river narrows, and little swirls
moving along the surface turn into whirlpools near the cliff. Stunted trees and
shrubs cling to the ledges, yet climbing out of the water seems challenging
there. However, currents have piled up fine sand at the eastern bank, where it
seems possible to even land a boat.
name: The Swirling Anduin

desc:
The ancient trail turns south as it approaches the river Anduin from the east,
so that only a few feet of grassy bank are left between it and the water. On the
other side of the trail, grassy plains, a home for many wild animals, continue
to the horizon and probably even further. The high walls of reeds, which seem to
be growing very well on the northern bank of the river, slowly become less dense
and finally disappear as the river turns south again. The trail itself is slowly
being taken over by small bushes and other low vegetation.
name: Overgrown Trail above the Anduin

desc:
A narrow trail leads over the cliffs bordering the riverbed, making a sharp turn
at the edge of an escarpment overlooking the river. On a clear day, the other
bank and the lands far to the north might be visible from there. In the north
below the cliff, tall bushes cover the ground just before the river, but they
would not stop one from breaking some bones with the fall. The trail continues
above the steep rock to the east, and in the south it passes close to a dark
hole through the cliff before disappearing into brushwood.
name: Trail with a View over the River

desc:
Having flown past a muddy armlet, the waters of the great Anduin slowly clear
here. The bottom of the river, covered with rocks and piles of pebbles, is
clearly visible through the water. Garbled red weeds growing on a few rocks, at
the bottom of the riverbed, give the water a strange pink tone. A worn trail on
the eastern bank follows the river as it keeps turning west. In the south, some
bushes grow on the riverbank, but behind them a vertical face of rock prevents
climbing on the high bank.
name: The Anduin River

desc:
An old trail, overgrown with grass, comes to an end here at the steep cliffs
outlining the river Anduin. Only a few bent twigs on the path indicate that it
is still being used these days. The trail fades into the grass and finally
disappears amidst the dense bushes at the edge of the riverside cliffs. The
ground south of the trail becomes slightly rocky and sandy as it slopes down
towards a distant bend in the river. North of the trail, neither rocks nor sand
can be seen, as green grass dominates the landscape together with a huge beech
standing only a few yards away. Heading west through the grasslands, the trail
starts its long journey towards Lorien.
name: At the End of the Grassy Trail

desc:
The bushes in this part of the forest are not very dense, yet moving among the
trees without cracking branches or stumbling over roots seems hard for the
unskilled. Mixed woods turn into a grove of ashes in the west, while in the
north only young birches grow on the other side of a dried-up stream. The forest
continues far to the east before it meets the edge of widespread, barren plains.
name: Brushwood Bordering a Grove

desc:
Ashes with burly trunks and far-reaching branches dominate this part of the
forest. Dead wood, rocks, everything on the ground is covered in thick moss and
little else; only in spots where sunlight can directly shine through the trees,
ivies crawling up along the trunks can be noticed. A single man-sized rock
stands erect in the light of a small opening; elsewhere the twilight and the
trees conceal similar shapes, making the forest seem more lively than it
actually is. The terrain soon gets much harder to travel towards the deeper
parts of the forest; coming back out could prove to be extremely difficult.
name: Grove of Ancient Ashes

desc:
This narrow trench lies deep in the middle of a mound of head-sized rocks. Some
wooden supports have been set against the neatly piled stones forming the walls,
which seem stable enough to allow moving here safely. The trench is covered from
above by some dry, leafless branches, while a mat of weeds torn off the nearby
rocks has been dragged here to seal the entrance. The surrounding fields and the
sky above can be seen though the dead vegetation and some loose stones, but
there is no doubt this place is hidden well from the outside. Rusty chips of
metal and a small piece of fabric on the ground hint at supplies being stored
here at times.
name: A Hidden Cache

desc:
The rocky tunnel leading down from the cliffs above gets wider when it reaches
the water level, then expands into a small cavern several feet under the
surface. Water stands still in the middle of the tunnel, but some algae near a
gap in the rocky wall are swaying in the current. In the far corner of the
underwater cavern, a wide tunnel leads away from the river.
name: An Underwater Tunnel

desc:
Water trickles through a wide crack in the rough wall of this circular cavern. A
gentle breeze passes through the crack, but the air down here is stale and
smells like rotten fish. On the muddy cavern floor, tiny insects scurry away
from droplets of water falling from the ceiling. At one end of the cavern, a
quiet pool turns into a tunnel leading towards the river.
name: A Muddy Cavern

desc:
A network of thin roots forms the ceiling of this somewhat cramped earthen
burrow. The ground here is very sandy, and all kinds of tracks are visible on
the dust-covered floor. It seems like this hole was dug a long time ago by some
animals, perhaps a fox couple. The markings on the walls and the debris lying
around suggest this sheltered dwelling has had many different inhabitants since.
The hole is probably larger nowadays than it originally was, since at a few
spots it is large enough for a man to stand up straight.
name: Inside a Den

desc:
This is the cave of a big animal. There is no telling who built the cave, but
the current inhabitants certainly do not want to share it. The air is musty and
pieces of half-eaten meat have drawn the attention of insects and rats. The
earthen ground is quite smooth.
name: A Bear's Den

desc:
The tunnel leading through the roots seems narrower near the base of the tree,
but down here it looks wide enough for a bear to sleep in. Sand has fallen off
the ceiling and walls at many spots, but the roots sticking out of the soil
everywhere have kept the tunnel from collapsing. Some of the larger roots have
holes running through them, and the whole place is crawling with insects.
name: A Deep Hole under the Tree

desc:
A set of planks has been bound together to form the floor of this small flet,
supported by the solid branches of the ancient beech. There are no shelves,
tables or other furniture here, suggesting that if this dwelling ever was
inhabited, those days are now long past. Some dirt is noticeable on the
floorboards, however; someone has been here only recently, otherwise the soil
would have been wiped away by the last rain. Also, a light curtain woven of
sturdy reeds hangs on one branch, perhaps to give some protection against cold
winds or bright rays of the sun. Even the ladder leading down, while hard to
notice, seems to have been in constant use.
name: A Flet in the Great Beech

desc:
The wide branches of this ancient beech seem to retain their strength even
farther away from the trunk, allowing anyone with some climbing skills to move
among them with ease. Below these old branches, the cliff at the edge of the
river Anduin suddenly ends, followed by a fall of about sixty feet straight down
onto the water. Some of the branches even reach far past the rockface, granting
an impressive view of the river for anyone daring to climb that far. The foliage
looks thick enough to catch any sudden gusts of wind but also stops one from
discerning details in the surrounding landscape.
name: Out on the Branches

desc:
This woodland trail is narrow but well-trodden. The earth is a rich, fertile
shade of brown, spotted here and there with rounded grey stones. The border
between trail and forest is sudden and distinct - rich colours dazzle the eyes
as organic browns give way to the lush greens of the grasses and the pale blue
of flowers. Whoever made this trail, which continues to the south, clearly
intended to disturb the natural environment as little as possible.
name: A Woodland Trail

desc:
Dark grey rocks cover this bank of the great Anduin. Some of them lie in the
shallow water, where the droppings of some birds colour their surfaces with
lighter tones. The ancient trail follows the riverbed at a distance, bending at
a few points to avoid some of the rocky areas. Towards the east, the dirt of the
trail slowly becomes covered under a layer of scattered pebbles.
name: Trail Passing a Rocky Riverbank

desc:
Tufts of hardy grass grow here and there on this ancient trail, trying to thrive
in the hard-packed soil. A layer of white pebbles covers most of the trail
making it hard for trees and bushes to take root in it. Some trees can be seen
in the surroundings, but the way along the riverside fields here is free of
obstacles; it could be the trail is as easy to travel as it ever was. Some
animal paths lead from the river towards the forest in the southeast.
name: A Pebble-strewn Trail at the Forestside

desc:
An incessant rumbling and drumming can be heard as turbulent waters crash into
the rocks below. From this vantage point, the mighty Anduin River can be seen
for miles, rapidly approaching from the north but then forced westwards by this
raised terrain, before pushing on southwards once-more. A crisp fluvial scent
drifts with the enduring zephyrs which are so common near the river.
name: Bend in a Pebble-strewn Trail

desc:
The river shimmers blue and is so clear nearer the riverbank, that the fine
pebbles and underwater vegetation are visible beneath the transparent waters.
Arcing westward, the river flows away from an impassable cliff to the east.
name: Bend in the Great River Anduin

desc:
Near the banks at this bend of the river, there are big gloomy rocks hiding in
the rushing water. Where the flow of the water is swiftest, it looks very
dangerous for small boats and rafts, while in the middle of the river the water
looks dark and quite deep. Small whirls are moving quickly along the surface of
the Great River there.
name: Rocks in the Anduin

desc:
The Anduin flows in from the east and continues westward. A number of wooden
beams float in the river, gathered together in the middle of a whirlpool. The
swirling water also plays with some ropes and splinters stuck between the nearby
rocks, dragging everything around in an endless dance. All this looks like the
remains of a big raft. To the west the river seems somewhat calmer.
name: At a Broken Raft in the River Anduin

desc:
Deeper and narrower than most parts of the Anduin, the riverbed bends past a
small bluff in the south. Low grass is growing on it, adding to the smoothness
of its outline. Dense green weeds float on the water, covering almost half of
the river; their tiny pink blossoms sway gently above the surface.
name: Dense Weeds in the River Anduin

desc:
Swiftly the water, pure and clean, flows by. Just a barely noticeable green
tone, caused by water plants, hinders the visibility through water. The
riverbanks are grassy, with neither rocks nor sand covering them. They meet the
surface of the water in a steep angle, making the riverbed seem quite deep
nearby.
name: The Swift Anduin

desc:
Slowly ascending westwards, the meadow climbs up towards a grassy hillock. The
green grass, with a slightly mossy cover, is full of various sparkling flowers
and smaller bushes. The sight here is truly motley. Plains and grasslands
continue in all directions.
name: A Grassy Meadow

desc:
This plain is covered with an abundance of long thriving grass and some small
shrubs and bushes. Here are no trails, but to the west a long wall of grey
stones separates the plain from more cultivated land. A small wooden gate is set
in the wall.
name: In Front of a Garden Gate

desc:
This stretch of land is abandoned farmland. Dotted around are many traces of the
former inhabitants, like old field enclosures and old cart tracks that are
deeply cut into the soil and still not overgrown. The most prominent trace of
former inhabitation are the dense hedges that prevent travel to the north and
west.
name: A Corner between Hedges

desc:
The grass grows long in this huge plain. A few shrubs and bushes provide shelter
from the sometimes strong winds blowing over the plain land. Animal life seems
to be rare, but there are a few tracks from small animals that can be hidden in
the high grass.
name: A Grassy Plain

desc:
Between a stretch of ancient farmland to the west and a dense hedge of hazels to
the east lies a strip of land that is easily recognisable as an old field. Some
patches of the old crop can be found amidst the usual long grass. No bushes or
shrubs have made their roots here.
name: An Old Field

desc:
Like all the terrain between the Misty Mountains and the Anduin this area is a
plain, flat land covered with grass. There is only grass visible all around.
There is absolutely nothing interesting in the landscape that could catch an
eye.
name: A Boring Plain

desc:
Grass covers the land, with small dots of shrubs and bushes interrupting the
monotony of the grassland, but there are no trees that could provide shade.
Westwards the land begins to rise and form small hills. Southwards the bushes
grow denser until a strong hedge makes further travel impossible.
name: A Grassy Plain

desc:
The plain grassland begins to change into soft sloped hills here. Between some
of the small hills and north of a hazel bush hedge some traces of former
inhabitation can be found. The stone foundation of a longhouse with a central
heath and two sheds looks like a long abandoned farm. Grass has grown over the
foundations.
name: An Abandoned Farm

desc:
The way further south is blocked by a grey wall of fine set stones and to the
east and west long rows of dense planted hazel bushes form impassable hedges.
Once upon a time a similar hedge to the north made this grassy plain an enclosed
corral, but there is a hole in it where once there was a gate.
name: A Dead End between Hazels

desc:
This brushland is a rather uncommon sight since the ground under the small,
light bushes growing here is covered by a bumpy layer of velvety green moss.
This layer extends over the fields with hardly any gaps or loose patches,
indicating that large animals rarely stray from the few paths crossing this
area. The fragile moss could easily be torn off, revealing the sandy soil below.
The landscape descends slowly towards the river in the southeast.
name: Mossy Plains of Shrubs

desc:
In the middle of this big plain of grass grows a huge lonely oak. Its trunk is
enormous and its tree-top is thick with branches thicker than other trees
trunks. This tree seems to be old enough to see the first elves arrive. South
from the tree the mighty Anduin River flows with a strong current, its waves
topped with small crowns of white foam.
name: A Solitary Large Tree

desc:
Long grass covers the huge plain. Beautiful flowers including sword lilies,
dandelions and marigolds grow among the grass. The earth is soft, dark and wet,
it must be fertile. To the west a big tree towers over the plain land. Long
weeds grow in the water of the Anduin to the south.
name: A Wet Grassland

desc:
Much of the rich green grass here has been cropped short by grazing animals who
have nosed through the verdure, selecting only the most luscious leaves, grasses
and other flora. Brush lines the base of a slope at the edge of the meadow and
slowly gives way to clumps of sedge and sharp-leaved reeds on wet low ground.
This leads down into a marshy inlet choked with tall reeds.
name: Lea by the River

desc:
Lush meadows of green-glossed grasses cover the ground. The land rises and falls
slightly, like a green silk, caught by a gentle breeze. All around, tiny flowers
provide embroidery for this vast, flourishing carpet. The air smells wet, the
Anduin River flows not far to the southeast.
name: Tranquil Meadows

desc:
This is a slight depression in the land, which forms a very shallow but still
perceptible valley. The verdant and plush grasses form a rich tapestry, which
furls and unfurls as the land undulates all around. The small bowl is surrounded
by variegated shades of green and yellow, grass, flowers and sky all merging
into a prismatic feast for the eyes.
name: A Shallow Valley

desc:
The plains are full of plant life here. The grass is very long and is in intense
green colour. Even a soft breeze will move it and create waves of rolling grass.
A whispering murmur leads to a very small streamlet appearing under some stones.
It flows east to join the great river Anduin.
name: A Tiny Streamlet in the Plains

desc:
The earth of this plain land is thick, dark and wet. It must be fertile. The
grass is very long here and many colourful flowers grow between them. From time
to time old abandoned fields can be discovered where once settlers made this
land a larder.
name: A Fertile Plain

desc:
Like most of the land between the Anduin River to the east and the Misty
Mountains to the west this area is flat and fertile. The vegetation consists of
a huge variety of grasses and flowers, low shrubs and bushes. To the south there
is a big tree. A dense hedge prevents travel to the west.
name: A Grassland

desc:
The soil in this flatland is dark and wet. The ground is deep and very muddy,
every move makes a sucking noise. Looking around, there is grassland in all
directions. To the east the grass looks longer and greener, while to the far
south a huge lonely tree is visible.
name: A Muddy Flat

desc:
This land was once a well tended and very productive garden. Making full use of
the fertile soil the gardeners grew many different plants, but today no one
cares for the garden. A solid stone wall with a gate in the eastern wall
protected the garden from unwelcome visitors and still gives a good protection
against strong winds and grazing animals. In the protection of the wall some
garden plants still grow and flourish.
name: An Untended Garden

desc:
This is a small platform of woven branches and twigs, an elven-build talan with
only one moveable wall to shelter it from the wind. The flet looks uninhabited,
but not abandoned. A few provisions are stored at the foot of the moveable wall.
A small ladder hidden in the hollow trunk of the tree allows departure from the
flet.
name: On a Flet

desc:
A pattern of old hedges and knee-high stone walls covers this area. Old paths
between the enclosures, the occasional growth of farm crops and an old fountain
mark this land as a former farmland. Now the wild plant life slowly conquers the
land back and covers the remains of civilisation with a green cloak.
name: Old Farmland

desc:
Along the northern border of this stretch of land grows an immense hedge of
hazel bushes. Beside this hedge the land is covered by the typical brown
grasses, some low bushes and small groups of tousled trees. Tracks of small
animals show that the area is not totally deserted.
name: A Plain South of a Hedge

desc:
Dense and long grass covers this land. The soil seems to be very fertile, but
this plain lacks all plant life that needs deep roots. Only measly trees and a
few bushes can be discovered. It looks like something in the ground prevents
them from taking root.
name: A Grassy Plain

desc:
The land is flat. It is covered only by grass and a few patches of thin shrubs.
Taking a closer look on the grass a great variety of grass species and tracks of
small animals that probably hide in the high grass can be found.
name: A Flat Plain

desc:
The land here is fertile, but no one lives here to farm it. Everywhere grow
sword lilies and other flowers dotting the long, rich grass. Further east the
mighty Anduin River flows. A little slope leads to a small garden and to the
north there is a small cabin built of logs.
name: A Plain of Flowers

desc:
This broad landscape is sparsely adorned with thorny trees, many of which have
had their succulent, nourishing lower leaves ravished - their bare branches
indicate a healthy population of wild deer and similar grazers. Barring these
few trees, the ground is wide and open and cloaked with tall, yellow grass.
Strangely enough, the dry foliage in this region is evidently enough to support
a wealth of wildlife.
name: Yellow Grassland

desc:
Here the tall hardy grasses of the plainsland in the west gradually give way to
an increasing proliferation of bushes and shrubs, forming a dense blanket over
the ground. Conversely, this region is considerably less colourful than the
bright, green, flower-covered grasslands in the east.
name: Vast Scrubland

desc:
This large treeless stretch of grassland reaches far in all directions and is
enhanced by a spattering of many-coloured flowers. An earthy scent combines with
the softness of the blades of grass to create a peaceful atmosphere. The mighty
Anduin River flows south and east, making the plains fertile.
name: A Small Rise in the Plains

desc:
The ground is very soft and wet in this place. Tall brown reeds grow in dense
patches. The great river must be very near, and it might be possible this area
becomes flooded if the river swells in the spring or after strong rainfalls.
name: A Muddy Sinkhole

desc:
Staleness clings to the air as much as the thick layers of dirt cling to
everything else in this small room. Shiny gossamer nets hang in the room's
corners where their arachnid hosts may eagerly await tasty guests. The pungent
odour of rotten plants and mud lingers here. The floor is covered with a thick
layer of dirt and mud, broken by deep pools of dark water. A chest that looks
much newer stands in one corner.
name: Hole below the Mud

desc:
A myriad of stems struggle to grow between the grey rocks, their leaves and
flowers reaching desperately for the open air above. This natural, raised
embankment affords a panoramic view of the surroundings. To the north, behind a
deep gully, is a small rise and on the eastern and southern flanks, the Great
River meanders ever onwards. A peaceful meadow flourishes in the west.
name: Rocky Levee

desc:
Scattered about are the weathered remains of various tools. Probably once used
to sift through the gravel and soil for gemstones, they have been long since
abandoned. Over time, the mighty Anduin has deposited this alluvium here -
fertile soil and a potential for gems that were released from their source rocks
by aeons of erosion. The local grassland around this soil bed is of little
interest, but to the west is a small rise in the endless plains.
name: Alluvial Soil Bed

desc:
A profusion of colourful flowers seem to explode from the ground and attract a
multitude of small insects. These colourful fields are home to countless
creatures. Bursting buds offer themselves to the passing insects which visit
each flower briefly before moving on.
name: Tapestry of Blossoms

desc:
This area began as a side loop of the river that swayed back and forth, as
flowing water does when traversing open land. Eventually, the loop closed back
on itself and filled, to form a small, shallow lake, which was isolated when the
river changed course. Many plants thrive in this now-rich meadow, with
water-loving bog plants nearer the river, where some stagnant water remains. The
water of the lake gives off a slight swampy odour.
name: Drying Oxbow Lake

desc:
Loosely clustered upon the banks of the Anduin River, these trees are covered in
rich, dark olive-green masses of foliage and overhang golden beds of
marsh-marigolds, or, later in the year, the foamy banks of meadow-sweet and the
magenta spikes of the loose-strife. Like all water-side plants, they retain
their leaves longer than the deciduous trees of drier regions.
name: Gathering of Alder Trees

desc:
In every direction, a ubiquity of green stretches to the edges of the horizon.
The spongy grass is like a vast cushion, soothing to travel. Traversing these
steppes is an interminable process due to the unchanging landscape. However,
many creatures probably migrate through this area between seasons.
name: Grassy Steppes

desc:
The grassland lowers to the southeast where the mighty Anduin River flows. The
nearby stream has its influence on the vegetation in this area. The ground is
fertile. The grass grows very tall and has an intense green colour. Scattered
over the plains are some groups of alders. Some miles to the north begins a
forest, in all other directions is open land.
name: Rich and Fertile Plains

desc:
The area is an endless expanse of arid terrain. The parched earth yearns to be
quenched. Here and there a tenacious shrub clings to life. These rugged plants
are few and far between, dotting the landscape.
name: Barren Expanse

desc:
Scatterings of scraggy, grey trees are all that decorate this bleak, rocky
terrain. Their gnarled, torrid branches are naked as if the broiling, merciless
sun seared away all life. The whine of blood-drinking insects vibrates in the
air, but there is usually enough of a breeze to keep them to a minimum.
name: Desolate Waste

desc:
A few square stone settings mark old patches where someone grew herbs,
vegetables and flowers a long time ago. Missing the hands of a gardener the beds
are overgrown by the grass now. A slope to the south leads to a flowered plain
and the backside of a log-cabin. It seems to be in much better shape than the
garden.
name: A Long Abandoned Garden

desc:
The land between the Misty Mountains and the Anduin is covered by grasses and
small bushes. It is fertile but lonely. Looking around there are traces of
former inhabitants like overgrown fields and gardens. To the east a small
structure in the endless grassland can be seen.
name: A Flat Plain

desc:
This land looks like the surrounding area. It is covered by long grass and
dotted with some small bushes. A group of trees that are larger than the average
birches catches the eye and leads to a small pond of brown water in a dell. The
banks of the pond are muddy and littered with rotten plant material. Some
rotting wood is floating in the water too.
name: A Small Pond

desc:
Bushes, small groups of tousled birches and an abundance of different grasses
cover the land and show its fertility. Despite the very rich soil there are no
settlements, only grassland as far as one can see. A hedge blocks the way to the
south.
name: A Grassy Plain

desc:
A beehive shaped stone structure crowns the top of a small hill in the grass
plains. It looks ancient, but the hard stone is untouched by the ravages of
time. The north side of the structure is covered with mosses. A tablet with a
withered inscription is set in the stone wall there.
name: An Ancient Tomb

desc:
Long brown grasses cover most of this area. A few soft hills dot the land and
provide some shelter from the winds. Hardy bushes and occasional birches grow in
the few protected spots. A small stone structure stands on a hill to the south.
name: A Grassy Plain

desc:
The plain looks borderless. Long thriving brown grass covers the land. When wind
comes up, it bends and rolls in waves. There are no landmarks or traces of
settlements with the notable exception of a sharp cliff edge that blocks travel
northwards.
name: Vast Plains

desc:
The plains in this area are marked by numerous small rocks, stones which are
scattered between the grass and some thin shrubs and bushes. The rocks and
stones are withered by the forces of nature, the vegetation seems sickly. The
lands north and south look more fertile. East are some ruins, to the west is
plain land.
name: Rock-strewn Plains

desc:
A stonewall which has now many holes in it was once the border of a holy site.
The shrine is a roofless ruin now. Only a corner of the building protecting the
remains of the statue of a goddess can be recognised. The other walls were
reduced to piles of stones by an unknown force. Grasses and mosses grow in the
cracks between the stones. The place is surrounded by plains. An alley of trees
leads northwards.
name: Destroyed Shrine

desc:
Many flowers grow among the thriving grass that covers this plain. The grass is
very long; it looks like the soil in this area is very fertile, although the
area lacks larger plants like bushes or trees. The plain land continues in all
directions.
name: Fragrant Plains

desc:
The terrain changes from verdant green to a grey, dried out brown. The soil
seems unable to support plant life. Only the hardiest mosses and weeds find a
living in cracks of the ground. The lack of plants is made up by a surplus of
bloodsucking insects that make this plain inhospitable.
name: Desolate Plains

desc:
Brown and dry grass covers this land. Very few other plants grow between the
reeds, and most of them are stinging nettles. The earth is hard and burnt. Some
small knee-high stone walls, dried out trenches and the ruin of a fence show
this land was once much more fertile, but an unknown force eroded it. Some
ruined building that might be the relics of a farmhouse can be seen to the
north.
name: Plains of Nettles

desc:
There is an abundance of plant life in this area, but there are no useful plants
to find. Everywhere grow thorny bushes, and between them are patches of thistles
and nettles. The place looks unfriendly and inhospitable.
name: Field of Thornbushes

desc:
This small hut is built of logs, straw and earth. A small fireplace provides
heating and makes this hut a comfortable shelter. A pile of tattered furs,
obviously the bed of the inhabitant, is the only furniture here.
name: A Small Hut in the Plains

desc:
Large rugs and bear furs cover the ground inside this pavilion. The tent itself
is made out of large canvas pieces sewn together. A large mast stands in the
middle of the pavilion and several bracing wires and support poles help to keep
the tent erect even in harsh weather conditions. To one side of the tent, below
a vent, are the ashes of a campfire. Near it a table and a few chairs have been
placed. To the other side of the pavilion stand a few crates and barrels, and
there is a line stretched between two poles. In the back lies a warm-looking
sleeping fur against one of the poles, and several cushions and blankets lie
piled beside it. A corpse, rotting away in his clothes, hangs from the line,
with a long slit through the throat.
name: Rich Tent

desc:
The Anduin is wide here, with its waters swollen. It approaches a marshy inlet
to the west, where twelve-foot phragmite reeds reach up out of the murky waters,
before winding southwards. Above the quagmire is a green lea.
name: Bend in the Great River Anduin

desc:
The water here is foamy after crashing into the cliffs visible to the east.
Meandering steadily southwest, the mighty Anduin deposits much of its load of
silt just west of here in an alluvial bed. From there on the waters appear much
clearer. When sunlight or moonlight hits the water it sparkles like dark silk
covered with diamonds.
name: Bend in the Great Anduin

desc:
There appears to be a trail above the southern cliff, here, but no details can
be made out from this angle. The river flows slowly past the grove to the east,
but picks up speed greatly as it rushes down toward the cliff and explodes
against it, producing a deafening roar.
name: Bend in the River Anduin below a Trail

desc:
The fresh waters of the Anduin begin to calm here, with the rapids left behind
in the east. To the west, a rocky embankment ushers the majestic, royal blue
river to the south, away from the alluvial beds of the north - and the shame of
its previously dirty, silt-saturated state.
name: Bend in the Great River

desc:
The golden forest stretches out in all directions except to the east, where it
gives way to a mixed forest of elm trees, oaks, ashes and rowans. In that
direction the trees are mostly brown, green and grey, but the glow of the Golden
Wood lies about them like the merry firelight from a hearth lit during a feast
in an elven hall.
name: The Golden Wood

desc:
Set in a narrow pass between two steep, rugged cliffs, these ancient stone
stairs descend towards the Great River flowing by in the east. Each step is at
least four feet wide, allowing one to haul goods or lead mounts down to a
sheltered bay which seems like an abandoned landing for boats. The roughly hewn
stone of the steps is covered in a carpet of fragile grass and lichen, yet at
some points, intricate engravings are faintly visible below the growth. The
staircase bends slightly, and together with the high walls of rock, it offers
shade during the daytime. Some lengths of iron rail, attached to the rock, seem
sturdy despite their apparent age, and offer a firm handhold for climbing the
stairs.
name: Ancient Stairs to the River Anduin

desc:
Drawn by their own weight, the stone structures of this quay are slowly sinking
into the mud of the river. Being abandoned, almost the whole quay has been
surrounded by walls of reeds, only the very end reaches out to the more open
part of the Anduin. The greater part of the quay has already sunk under a few
feet of water and is growing algae. All kinds of shallow-water plants are
thriving here despite being shaded by the cliffs above.
name: Partly Submerged Quay

desc:
The Anduin widens considerably here, and the riverbed becomes quite shallow.
Probably because of that, the vegetation in the water is very dense, consisting
of reeds and clusters of blossoming plants with large leaves. Still, the bottom
of the river cannot be seen here; the water is quite brown and full of drifting
stems and leaves. Even the banks are hidden behind the walls of reeds for the
most part.
name: Shallows in the Anduin

desc:
The course of the mighty river follows a straight path from north to south,
rushing rapidly past verdant riverbanks. The river is wide and in most places
very deep, although clusters of partially submerged boulders near the eastern
shore indicate shallower waters in that direction. To the west, the woodlands
are dense and rise high upon upward sloping ground.
name: In the Strong Current
